Bradfute, Charles H. Calisher, Boris Klempa, Jonas Klingström, Jens H. Kuhn, Lies Laenen, Nicole D. Tischler and Piet Maes The citation for this ICTV Report chapter is the summary published as: Bradfute, S. B., Calisher, C. H., Klempa, B., Klingström, J., Kuhn, J. H., Laenen, L., Tischler, N. D., & Maes, P. (2024). ICTV Virus Taxonomy Profile: Hantaviridae 2024, Journal of General Virology 105, 001975 Corresponding author: Piet Maes ( [email protected] ) Edited by: Holly Hughes and Evelien Adriaenssens Posted: March 2024, updated June 2024 Summary Hantaviridae is a family for negative-sense RNA viruses with genomes of about 10.5–14.6 kb (Table 1 Hantaviridae ). The family includes eight genera ( Actinovirus and Percilovirus (subfamily Actantavirinae ), Agnathovirus (subfamily Agantavirinae ), Loanvirus , Mobatvirus , Orthohantavirus and Thottimvirus (subfamily Mammantavirinae ), and Reptillovirus (subfamily Repantavirinae ). These viruses are maintained in and/or transmitted by small mammals, but also by fish and reptiles. Several orthohantaviruses can infect humans, causing mild, severe, and sometimes-fatal diseases. Hantavirids produce enveloped virions containing three single-stranded RNA segments with open reading frames (ORFs) that encode a nucleoprotein (N), a glycoprotein precursor (GPC), and large (L) protein containing an RNA-directed RNA polymerase (RdRP) domain. Table 1 Hantaviridae. Characteristics of members of the family Hantaviridae. Characteristic Description* Example Hantaan virus (S segment: M14626; M segment: M14627; L segment: X55901), species Orthohantavirus hantanense Virion Enveloped, pleomorphic virions 80–160 nm in diameter with heterodimeric surface spikes Genome Three single-stranded RNA molecules (segments): small (S; 1.0–3.0 kb), medium (M; 3.4–4.8 kb), and large (L; 5.3–6.8 kb) Replication Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes containing anti-genomic RNA serve as coding templates for synthesis of genomic RNA Translation Proteins are produced from capped and non-polyadenylated mRNAs. The 5′-cap structure is obtained via cap-snatching from cellular mRNAs Host range Fish (actinoviruses, agnathoviruses, perciloviruses), mammals (orthohantaviruses), and reptiles (reptilloviruses) Taxonomy Realm Riboviria , kingdom Orthornavirae , phylum Negarnaviricota , class Bunyaviricetes , order Elliovirales ; the family includes four subfamilies, eight genera, and 55 species * mostly based on experiments with mammalian orthohantaviruses Viruses assigned to each of the seven genera form genus-specifc monophyletic clades based on phylogenetic analysis of concatenated L protein/RdRP, glycoprotein and N sequences. Viruses from all seven genera share one or more of the following characteristics: (i) enveloped pleomorphic virions; (ii) tri-segmented, negative-sense RNA genome without polyadenylated tracts at the 3′-end; (iii) genomic sequence complementarity at the 5′- and 3′-ends; and (iv) capped but not polyadenylated virus mRNAs ( Maes et al., 2004 ). Piscine Hosts Genus Actinovirus . Viruses in this genus infect actinopterygian (percid, ogcocephalid, and triacanthodid, and possibly goblid and melanotaeniid) fish. Genus Agnathovirus . Viruses in this genus infect agnathan (myxinid) fish. Genus Percilovirus . Viruses in this genus infect actinopterygian ( gobiid, lophiid ) fish. Mammalian Hosts Genus Loanvirus. Viruses in this genus infect rhinolophid, verspertillionid, and possibly nycterid bats and possibly murid rodents. Genus Mobatvirus. Viruses in this genus infect emballonurid, hipposiderid, pteropodid and possibly molossid, and vespertillionid bats and soricid and talpid eulipotyphla. Genus Orthohantavirus. Viruses assigned to this genus infect soricid and talpid eulipotyphla and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents. Genus Thottimvirus. Viruses in this genus infect soricid and possibly talpid eulipotyphla. Reptilian Hosts Genus Reptillovirus . Viruses in this genus infect gekkonid and possibly scincid reptiles. Virion Morphology Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Physicochemical and physical properties Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Nucleic acid Hantavirids have tri-segmented, negative-sense RNA genomes (S, M, and L segments). The viral mRNAs are not polyadenylated and contain a 5′-methylated cap and several non-templated nucleotides at the 5′-end that are derived from host cell mRNAs via cap snatching ( Schmaljohn et al., 1985 , Jonsson and Schmaljohn 2001 , Vaheri et al., 2013 ). Further information is only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Proteins Hantavirids typically express three structural proteins. The most abundant structural protein in a hantavirion is N, which encapsidates the hantaviral genomic segments. The least abundant protein is L, which mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Two glycoprotein subunits, G N and G C , are produced from GPC and mediate entry of hantavirions ( Schmaljohn et al., 1985 , Spiropoulou 2001 , Vaheri et al., 2013 ). Specifics are only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Lipids Not reported but are likely derived from the Golgi apparatus and/or the host cell plasma membrane and therefore likely composed of phospholipids, glycolipids, fatty acids, and sterols. Carbohydrates Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Genome organization and replication S RNA encodes N and in some viruses NSs ( Jääskeläinen et al., 2007 , Vera-Otarola et al., 2012 ), M RNA encodes GPC, and the L RNA encodes L with its RdRP, helicase, and endonuclease domains ( Jonsson and Schmaljohn 2001 ) (Figure 1 Hantaviridae ). Specifics are only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Figure 1 Hantaviridae . Genome organization of representative hantaviruses. The 5′- and 3′- ends of all segments are complementary at their termini, likely promoting the formation of panhandle RNP complexes within the virion . GPC , glycoprotein precursor gene; L , large (protein) gene; N , nucleoprotein gene. Biology Hantavirids have been found in actinopterygian (gobiid, lophiid, percid, ogcocephalid, and triacanthodid, and possibly goblid and melanotaeniid) and agnathan (myxinid) fish; in emballonurid, hipposiderid, pteropodid, rhinolophid, verspertillionid, and possibly molossid and nycterid bats; in soricid and talpid eulipotyphla; in muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents; in gekkonid and possibly scincid reptiles; and potentially in coleopteran and plectopteran insects (see genus pages for details). Only orthohantaviruses are known to cause diseases (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Antigenicity Systematic antigenicity studies have only been reported for orthohantaviruses (see Orthohantavirus genus page). England thailandense : after Thailand thottapalayamense : after Thottapalayam , India Thottimvirus : after Thott opalayam virus tigrayense : after ትግራይ ( Tigray Region), Ethiopia triacanthodis : after the fish genus Triacanthod es tulaense : after Тула ( Tula ), Russia xuansonense : after Xuân Sơn , Vietnam wufangense : after after Wùfēng (吾锋), Fújiàn Province (福建省), China Subfamily, genus and species demarcation criteria The availability of at least coding-complete sequences of all three genome segments may be sufficient for hantavirid classification in the absence of a cultured isolate. Demarcation of genera is based upon DivErsity pArtitioning by hieRarchical Clustering (DEmARC) analysis) using concatenated deduced S, M, and L segment expression product sequences ( Hantaviridae: current classification and future perspectives, Viruses, 11, 9, e0788">Laenen et al., 2019 ). DEmARC analysis gave a frequency distribution of Pairwise Evolutionary Distance (PED) values of which the threshold of 0.1 gave an optimal clustering cost of zero and is used as the hantavirid species demarcation criterium. Genera are demarked by a PED-value threshold of 0.95 and subfamilies are demarcated based on their distinct clustering in a Bayesian maximum clade credibility tree and a PED-value threshold of 3.5. Relationships within the family Phylogenetic relationships across the family have been estimated using Bayesian maximum clade credibility trees generated from complete N, GPC, and L amino-acid sequences; similar topologies are obtained with concatenated N and GPC sequences (Figure 2 Hantaviridae ). Figure 2 Hantaviridae . Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree inferred using IQ-TREE. The tree was constructed based on 53 concatenated S and M segment (amino acid) sequences using the LG+R9 model of sequence evolution. Branch lengths represent the estimated number of substitutions per site. Bootstrap support values (>70%) are indicated at nodes, reflecting the degree of confidence in the branching pattern. Relationships with other taxa Hantavirids are closely related to bunyaviral crulivirids, fimovirids, peribunyavirids, phasmavirids, tulasvirids, and tospovirids ( Wolf et al., 2018 , Herath et al., 2020 ). Related, unclassified viruses Virus name Accession number Virus abbreviation Reference coleopteran hanta-related virus OKIAV221 L: MT153497 ( Käfer et al., 2019 ) plecopteran hanta-related virus OKIAV215 L: MT153434 ( Käfer et al., 2019 ) Virus names and virus abbreviations are not official ICTV designations. Additional unclassified hantavirids that are probable members of established genera are listed under individual genus descriptions. Using the Master Species List Using the Virus Metadata Resource Using the Find the Species tool Using the ICTV Report Webinar Webinar on Virus Taxonomy Webinar on Finding Information GitHub Repository Zenodo Repository Email ICTV Help ‏‏‎ ‎ ICTV Report Book: Hantaviridae Family: Hantaviridae Subfamily: Actantavirinae Genus: Actinovirus Genus: Percilovirus Subfamily: Agantavirinae Genus: Agnathovirus Subfamily: Mammantavirinae Genus: Loanvirus Genus: Mobatvirus Genus: Orthohantavirus Genus: Thottimvirus Subfamily: Repantavirinae Genus: Reptillovirus Authors: Hantaviridae Citation: Hantaviridae References: Hantaviridae Resources: Hantaviridae Species List: Hantaviridae Subfamily: Mammantavirinae Genus: Orthohantavirus Distinguishing features Sixty different viruses are classified in the genus Orthohantavius. These viruses typically infect specific soricid and talpid eulipotyphla and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents. Orthohantaviruses are the only hantavirids known to cause disease in humans ( Schmaljohn and Hjelle 1997 , Jonsson et al., 2010 , Kuhn and Schmaljohn 2023 , Vial et al., 2023 ). Virion Morphology Orthohantavirions are pleomorphic in shape, mostly spherical of 80–160 nm in diameter ( Huiskonen et al., 2010 ), and surrounded by a membrane envelope that is decorated with glycoprotein (GP) spikes composed of G N and G C subunits (Figure 1 Orthohantavirus ). The virion envelope is 5 nm thick and is decorated with square shaped spikes, consisting of tetrameric G N -G C heterodimers, which protrude 10 nM from the membrane forming a surface lattice of local symmetry interrupted by empty membrane spots ( Hung et al., 1983 , Huiskonen et al., 2010 , Battisti et al., 2011 , Parvate et al., 2019 , Serris et al., 2020 , Guardado-Calvo and Rey 2021 ). Isolated ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes are composed of individual segment genomic RNA encapsidated by nucleoprotein (N) ( Tao et al., 1987 , Huiskonen et al., 2010 , Battisti et al., 2011 , Hepojoki et al., 2012 , Guo et al., 2016 , Arragain et al., 2019 ). Virions assemble and bud from the Golgi complex or cell surface membranes, depending on the virus ( Goldsmith et al., 1995 , Parvate et al., 2019 ). Figure 1 Orthohantavirus. A, B) Electron micrographs of Tula virus particles adapted from ( Huiskonen et al., 2010 ). C) Schematic illustration of an orthohantavirus particle. Shown is a spherical and enveloped (grey) particle with glycoprotein spikes (G N yellow, G C blue) inserted in a bilaminar lipid envelope. The S (small), M (medium), and L (large) RNP (ribonucleoprotein) complexes inside the particle consist of N (nucleoprotein, green) and L (large protein, pink). ViralZone image reproduced courtesy of SwissBioPics. Physicochemical and physical properties Virion buoyant densities in sucrose and CsCl are 1.15–1.18 ( White et al., 1982 ) and 1.20–1.21 g cm −3 , respectively ( Schmaljohn et al., 1983 ). Virions are sensitive to chemical inactivation with, for instance, methanol, paraformaldehyde, acetone/methanol, N -ethylmaleimide or detergents, as well as physical methods such as heat, gamma irradiation or UV irradiation ( Kraus et al., 2005 , Strandin et al., 2011 , Elveborg et al., 2022 ). Nucleic acid Orthohantavirus genomes are linear tri-segmented RNA molecules of negative polarity. The genomes are about 11.1–12.3 kb in length (small [S] segment: 1.2–2.1 kb; medium [M] segment: 3.4–3.8 kb; large [L] segment: 6.5–6.6 kb). Proteins Orthohantaviruses typically express three structural proteins (Table 1 Orthohantavirus ). The most abundant structural protein in an orthohantavirion is N, which encapsidates the orthohantaviral genomic segments. The least abundant protein is L, which mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Two glycoprotein subunits, G N and G C , mediate virion entry. G N and G C and non-structural proteins are encoded as a precursor polyprotein. Table 1 Orthohantavirus . Location and function of orthohantavirus structural proteins. Protein Location, mass, and function Reference Nucleoprotein (N) Structural virion protein (about 48 kD). Oligomerizes and encapsidates orthohantaviral genomic segments and hence component of RNPs. Binds to L and G N ( Mir et al., 2010 , Reuter and Krüger 2018 , Arragain et al., 2019 ) Nonstructural protein (NSs) Non-structural protein encoded by some orhtohantaviruses. Has type I interferon inhibitory activity. May occur as multiple isoforms. ( Vera-Otarola et al., 2020 , Binder et al., 2021 ) Glycoprotein (GP) Structural virion protein consisting of two subunits (G N : about 70-75 kD, G C : about 50-55 kD). Produced via proteolytic cleavage from the genome-encoded precursor GPC. Projects from virion membranes as tetrameric GP spikes composed of G N and G C heterodimers. GP mediates cell- receptor binding; as a class II fusion machine it induces virion-cell membrane fusion and, thereby cell entry. G N binds to N and RNA ( Spiropoulou et al., 2003 , Tischler et al., 2005 , Cifuentes-Muñoz et al., 2014 , Li et al., 2016 , Willensky et al., 2016 , Rissanen et al., 2017 , Zhu et al., 2018 , Bignon et al., 2019 , Sperber et al., 2019 ) Large protein (L) Structural virion protein (246 kD) with RdRP, helicase, and endoribonuclease domains. Component of the RNP inside virions. Binds to N and RNA. Oligomerizes and mediates transcription and replication of viral RNA segments. Mediates cap-snatching for viral mRNA capping. ( Kukkonen et al., 2005 , Cheng et al., 2014 , Rothenberger et al., 2016 , Jeeva et al., 2019 , Durieux Trouilleton et al., 2023 ) Genome organization and replication The orthohantavirus genome consists of three negative-sense RNA molecules, termed S (small), M (medium), and L (large) (Figure 2 Orthohantavirus ). Figure 2 Orthohantavirus. Schematic representation of orthohantavirus genome organization. Orthohantavirus genomic segments assume circular forms via non-covalent binding of complementary and highly conserved 3′- and 5′-terminal sequences. Genomic segment reassortment mostly occurs among viruses belonging to the same species but interspecies reassortment likely occurs occasionally ( Klempa 2018 , Petazzi et al., 2021 ). Orthohantavirus infection starts with virion attachment, mediated by G N and G C , to cell-surface attachment factors and entry via the endosomal route ( Shtanko et al., 2014 ) (Figure 3 Orthohantavirus ). Figure 3 Orthohantavirus . Lifecycle of orthohantaviruses. (1) Virion attachment; (2) virion uptake; (3) virion-cell membrane fusion; (4) transcription; (5) translation; (6) replication; (7) virion assembly; and (8) virion egress. After engaging a cellular receptor, protocadherin 1 in the case of American orthohantaviruses, virion-membrane fusion results in early or late endosomal release, depending on the virus, of the virion RNP complex into the cytoplasm in a pH-dependent manner ( Jangra et al., 2018 , Mittler et al., 2019 , Torriani et al., 2019 , Dieterle et al., 2021 ). During primary transcription L generates uncapped antigenomic RNA molecules that are then capped using host cell-derived capped primers (cap snatching). L and S segment-transcribed mRNAs are translated by free ribosomes ( Garcin et al., 1995 , Jeeva et al., 2019 , Meier et al., 2021 ). M segment-transcribed mRNA is translated by membrane-bound ribosomes, with the expressed GPC which is likely co-translationally cleaved by cellular proteases to yield G N and G C . The synthesis of the antigenome by L protein serves as a template for genomic RNA replication. Secondary transcription amplifies the synthesis of mRNAs and genome replication. During morphogenesis, G N and G C accumulate in the Golgi apparatus, modified host membranes are acquired, and virions bud into the Golgi. Biology Genus Orthohantavirus includes 38 species for 60 distinct viruses. These viruses and their unclassified, potential relatives, subclinically, and often chronically, infect specific soricid and talpid eulipotyphla, and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents ( Jonsson et al., 2010 , Wu et al., 2018 , Chen et al., 2023 , Kuhn and Schmaljohn 2023 ). Orthohantaviruses are overall distributed worldwide, but the individual geographic distribution is dependent on host range. At least some orthohantaviruses may not be as restricted to a particular host as originally thought, but may be carried by animals of multiple hosts ( Goodfellow et al., 2021 , Quizon et al., 2022 , Gu et al., 2023 ). Orthohantaviruses are the only hantavirids known to cause disease (all in humans) and all disease-causing viruses are rodent-borne. Human infection may occur after inhalation of aerosolized excreta or secreta of infected rodents or direct rodent contact. The two WHO-recognized orthohantavirus-caused diseases are hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), caused by Asian and European viruses (in particular Dobrava virus [DOBV], Hantaan virus [HTNV], Puumala virus [PUUV] and Seoul virus [SEOV]), and hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), a frequently lethal disease caused by viruses from the Americas (in particular Andes virus [ANDV] and Sin Nombre virus [SNV]) ( Vial et al., 2023 ). The hamster model of wild-type ANDV infection is lethal and largely mimics human HPS ( Hooper et al., 2001 ). After SNV infection in nonhuman primates, some animals develop an HPS-like disease, but cell passage of SNV can result in mutations that render the virus non-pathogenic in this model ( Safronetz et al., 2014 ). Many rodent models result in a chronic, asymptomatic infection, and these have been used in early-stage therapeutic studies ( Strandin et al., 2020 ). However, recent advances have been made in development of an HFRS mouse model using HTNV variants ( Wei et al., 2022 ). Orthohantavirus animal models are reviewed in ( Golden et al., 2015 ). Antigenicity There is considerable serum cross-reactivity between the nucleocapsids of hantavirids, but neutralizing antibodies targeting Gn/Gc are more specific. SNV was first identified as a hantavirid based on cross-reactivity of patient sera with other orthohantavirus antigens ( 1993 ). Similarly, rodents that carried SNV were identified in serosurveys using non-SNV orthohantavirus antigens, since SNV had not been isolated at the time ( Childs et al., 1994 ). Species demarcation criteria Demarcation of species is based upon DivErsity pArtitioning by hieRarchical Clustering (DEmARC) analysis) using concatenated deduced S, M, and L segment expression product sequences ( Laenen et al., 2019 ). Phylogenetic relationships across the genus have been estimated using maximum likelihood trees generated from complete N, GPC and L amino-acid sequences, and for N and GPC amino-acid sequences (Figure 2 Hantaviridae ). Hantaviridae ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Hantaviridae (taxid:1980413) VIRION Enveloped, spherical. Diameter from 80 to 120nm. GENOME Segmented Negative-stranded RNA linear genome, L segment is between 6.8 and 12 kb, M segment between 3.2 and 4.9 kb and S segment between 1 and 3 kb. Encodes for four to six proteins. Some hantaviruses also encode a NSs protein on their S segment. GENE EXPRESSION The viral RNA dependent RNA polymerase (L) binds to a promoter on each encapsidated segment, and transcribes the mRNA. These are capped by L protein during synthesis using cap snatching . Transcription is terminated by a strong hairpin sequence at the end of each gene. Puumala virus expressed an additional NSs by leaky scanning in the NS gene. ENZYMES RNA-dependent RNA polymerase L Cap Snatching endonuclease L REPLICATION CYTOPLASMIC Virus attaches to host receptors though Gn-Gc glycoprotein dimer, and is endocytosed into vesicles in the host cell. Fusion of virus membrane with the vesicle membrane; ribonucleocapsid segments are released in the cytoplasm. Transcription , viral mRNAs are capped in the cytoplasm. Replication presumably starts when enough nucleoprotein is present to encapsidate neo-synthetized antigenomes and genomes. The ribonucleocapsids buds at Golgi apparatus, releasing the virion by exocytosis. Orthohantavirus ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Orthohantavirus (taxid:1980442) Orthohantavirus is a genus of ssRNA- viruses in the Hantaviridae family that infect Rodents and rarely humans. In humans, these viruses can cause hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, or Hantavirus pulmonary syndromes. VIRION Enveloped, spherical. Diameter from 80 to 120nm. GENOME Segmented Negative-stranded RNA linear genome, L segment is between 6.8 and 12 kb, M segment between 3.2 and 4.9 kb and S segment between 1 and 3 kb. Encodes for four to six proteins. Some hantaviruses also encode a NSs protein on their S segment. GENE EXPRESSION The viral RNA dependent RNA polymerase (L) binds to a promoter on each encapsidated segment, and transcribes the mRNA. These are capped by L protein during synthesis using cap snatching . Transcription is terminated by a strong hairpin sequence at the end of each gene. mRNAs are capped by L protein during synthesis. Puumala virus expressed an additional NSs by leaky scanning in the NS gene. ENZYMES RNA-dependent RNA polymerase L Cap Snatching endonuclease L REPLICATION CYTOPLASMIC Virus attaches to host receptors though Gn-Gc glycoprotein dimer, and is endocytosed into vesicles in the host cell. Fusion of virus membrane with the vesicle membrane; ribonucleocapsid segments are released in the cytoplasm. Transcription , viral mRNAs are capped in the cytoplasm. Replication presumably starts when enough nucleoprotein is present to encapsidate neo-synthetized antigenomes and genomes. The ribonucleocapsids buds at Golgi apparatus, releasing the virion by exocytosis. The hantaviruses (Hantaviridae) ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Back to Andes virus resource There are approximately 92 known viruses in the family Hantaviridae, classified into 52 species. Each species is typically adapted to a specific reservoir host, most commonly rodents, but also bats and moles. With the notable exception of Andes virus, hantaviruses are generally not transmitted from person to person. Based on their geographic distribution, evolutionary relationships, and the diseases they cause in humans, hantaviruses can be broadly divided into three major groups: New World hantaviruses (the Americas) These viruses cause Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), a severe respiratory disease. Important examples include Sin Nombre virus and Andes virus. Annual incidence are about 100 cases per year (Andes virus) about 25 cases per year (Sin Nombre virus) Old World hantaviruses (Asia) These viruses are responsible for Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), which is characterized by fever, hemorrhage, and acute kidney injury. Major examples include Hantaan virus and Seoul virus. Annual incidence: more than 10,000 cases worldwide Old World hantaviruses (Europe) European hantaviruses typically cause a milder form of HFRS known as Nephropathia epidemica (NE). The main causative agents are Puumala virus and Dobrava-Belgrade virus. Annual incidence: more than 1,000 cases Together, these viruses represent an important and diverse group of zoonotic pathogens that can cause severe disease following accidental transmission from their natural animal hosts to humans.

papers.title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate
papers.abstract: Anthropogenic climate change is reshuffling global biodiversity and accelerating zoonotic spillover at the human-wildlife interface. The 2026 multi-country cruise ship outbreak of Andes virus highlights the unpredictable and globalized threat posed by rodent-borne orthohantaviruses, which cause severe, highly fatal human diseases worldwide. Despite this significant public health risk, macroecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to systemic shifts in hantavirus hazards has been lacking. Here, we implement an integrated ecological and machine learning framework combining 89 unique virus-host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic Force-of-Infection (FOI) modeling to project global spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) for 2041-2060. Our models demonstrate a spatially uneven but geographically extensive expansion of spillover hazards, with approximately 74.9% of modeled land area experiencing an increase in FOI. We identify three distinct high-hazard belts heavily concentrated in already-temperate reservoir ranges: western and central North America, central and northeastern Europe extending into western Russia, and discrete patches within central South America. Crucially, our findings reveal that hantavirus hazards are fundamentally shaped along virus-specific ecological axes. While thermal and precipitation anomalies predominate the transmission dynamics of Old and New World lineages like Sin-Nombre virus and Thailand virus, distinct urban-centric Profiles emerge for Seoul virus and Mamore virus, driven explicitly by fine-scale anthropogenic landscape modifications. These results reveal that climate change will not simply intensify viral spillover uniformly but will fundamentally restructure it across varied climatic and human-dominated environments. Our maps provide a critical baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight the urgent need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public health preparedness.

analysis.background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles global biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover (A1); the 2026 multi‑country cruise‑ship outbreak of Andes virus illustrates the unpredictable, global threat of rodent‑borne orthohantaviruses (A2); a macro‑ecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to hantavirus hazards has been lacking (A3).
analysis.methods: An integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework combined 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic force‑of‑infection (FOI) modeling to project global spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5) for 2041‑2060 (A4).
analysis.results: Models show a spatially uneven but extensive expansion, with ~74.9% of modeled land area experiencing increased FOI (A5); three high‑hazard belts are identified in western/central North America, central/eastern Europe into western Russia, and central South America (A6); hazards are shaped along virus‑specific ecological axes (A7); thermal and precipitation anomalies drive Old and New World lineages (e.g., Sin‑Nombre, Thailand virus) while urban‑centric profiles driven by fine‑scale anthropogenic changes affect Seoul and Mamore viruses (A8); climate change will restructure spillover across varied climatic and human‑dominated environments (A9).
analysis.contribution: The generated hazard maps provide a critical baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight the urgent need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public‑health preparedness (A10).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; detailed methods, data sources, validation procedures, and uncertainty estimates are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover, exemplified by the 2026 multi‑country cruise‑ship Andes virus outbreak, and a macro‑ecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to hantavirus hazards has been lacking. Methods: An integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework used 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic force‑of‑infection modeling to project spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2‑4.5, SSP5‑8.5) for 2041‑2060. Results: ~74.9% of land area is projected to have increased FOI, with three high‑hazard belts in western/central North America, central/eastern Europe into western Russia, and central South America; hazards align with virus‑specific ecological axes, with thermal/precipitation drivers for Old/New World lineages and urban‑centric profiles for Seoul and Mamore viruses. Contribution: The hazard maps offer a baseline for targeted global surveillance and underscore the need to merge spatial conservation planning with public‑health preparedness. Limitations: Only an abstract is available; detailed methods, data, validation, and uncertainty are not reported.

analysis_zh.background: 人为气候变化重塑全球生物多样性并加速人畜共患病溢出 (A1);2026 年多国邮轮疫情 安第斯病毒 展示了啮齿动物携带的 正汉坦病毒 的不可预测的全球威胁 (A2);缺乏将环境驱动因素与 汉坦病毒 危害联系起来的宏观生态综合研究 (A3)。
analysis_zh.methods: 一个综合生态与机器学习框架将 61 种储存宿主的 89 条病毒‐宿主关联与机制性感染强度 (FOI) 模型相结合,预测 2041‐2060 年在当前及未来气候情景 (SSP2‐4.5 与 SSP5‐8.5) 下的全球溢出危害 (A4)。
analysis_zh.results: 模型显示空间分布不均但范围广泛扩展,约 74.9% 的模型陆地区域出现 FOI 增加 (A5);在北美西部/中部、欧亚中部/东部至俄罗斯西部以及南美中部识别出三条高危带 (A6);危害沿病毒特异性生态轴形成 (A7);温度和降水异常驱动旧世界和新世界系谱(如 Sin‐Nombre、Thailand 病毒),而由细尺度人为变化导致的城市化特征影响首尔病毒和 Mamore 病毒 (A8);气候变化将重塑跨不同气候和人类主导环境的溢出格局 (A9)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 生成的危害图为有针对性的全球监测提供了关键基线,并凸显了将空间保护规划与公共卫生准备相结合的迫切需求 (A10)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供了摘要;未在所提供的证据中报告详细方法、数据来源、验证程序和不确定性估计。

papers.title: Spatiotemporal correlation analysis between climate change and hantavirus outbreak in Argentina: Quantitative causal inference based on Google Earth Engine and Bayesian spatiotemporal regression
papers.abstract: Objective: The number of confirmed cases of Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in Argentina surged to 101 in the 2025–2026 epidemic season, nearly doubling from the previous season. There was a significant geographical shift in the epicenter of the epidemic from the traditional Patagonian region to the province of Buenos Aires and the NOA region (Salta, Jujuy) in the northwest. This study aims to use the Google Earth Engine (GEE) cloud platform to integrate ERA5 reanalysis climate data and NOAA surface observation data, construct a Bayesian spatiotemporal hierarchy model, and quantitatively analyze the spatiotemporal correlation and causal path between climate anomalies (high temperature, drought, heavy rainfall) and hantavirus outbreaks. Methods: The data of confirmed cases of HPS from 2020 to 2026, released by the Argentine Ministry of Health (SNVS 2.0), WHO, and PAHO, were collected, and the geographical coordinates and time of onset of HPS were extracted. The ERA5-Land hourly dataset (0.1°×0.1° resolution) and NOAA Global Surface Summary of the Day (GSOD) site data were called by GEE to calculate the monthly temperature anomalies (TA), Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI), and Soil Moisture Anomaly (SMA), and frequency of extreme weather events. Bayesian Spatiotemporal Poisson Regression (Bayesian Spatiotemporal Poisson Regression) combined with the INLA (Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation) method was used to estimate the lag effect (lag of 1–6 months) and spatial heterogeneity of climate factors on the onset of HPS. Causal Mediation Analysis was used to identify the causal chains of "climate anomalies→ vegetation productivity→ rodent population expansion→ human exposure". Results: The spatial distribution of HPS cases in the 2025–2026 season showed significant unstable characteristics (Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001). Bayesian models showed that SPI (RR = 1.68, 95% CrI: 1.31–2.14) with a lag of 3–4 months (RR = 1.68, 95% CrI: 1.31–2.14) and TA (RR = 1.45, 95% CrI: 1.12–1.87 per 1°C increase) had a significant positive effect on the onset of HPS, and there was a synergistic interaction between the two (RERI = 0.79). Spatial regression revealed that the HPS risk in Buenos Aires was mainly associated with flood events after extreme rainfall (2.3-fold higher risk in flood months after SPI > 1.5), while outbreaks in NOA were closely related to sudden heavy rainfall ("drought-flood" compound extreme events) after persistent drought. Causal mediation analysis showed that the vegetation index (NDVI) played a significant mediating effect between climate anomalies and HPS risk (41.2% in Buenos Aires and 56.7% in the NOA region). Conclusion: This study realizes the quantitative causal inference of climate anomalies and hantavirus outbreaks at the national scale in Argentina for the first time, and confirms the causal pathway of the "drought-flood" compound extreme climate event driving rodent population expansion and human exposure risk increase through the trophic cascade. The GEE-based real-time monitoring framework can provide a methodological basis for the establishment of the HPS Climate-Driven Early Warning System in Argentina.
papers.journal: Journal of Public Health and Preventive Medicine

analysis.background: The 2025–2026 HPS epidemic in Argentina reached 101 confirmed cases, nearly double the previous season, with the epicenter shifting from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region.
analysis.methods: Case data (2020‑2026) with locations were combined via Google Earth Engine with ERA5‑Land temperature, SPI, and soil‑moisture anomalies; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression (INLA) evaluated 1‑6 month lag effects and spatial heterogeneity, and causal mediation analysis tested climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways.
analysis.results: Significant spatial clustering (Moran’s I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95 % CrI 1.31‑2.14) at 3‑4 month lag and temperature (RR = 1.45 per °C, 95 % CrI 1.12‑1.87) raised HPS risk, with interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood months increased Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold, drought‑followed‑heavy‑rainfall drove NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2 % and 56.7 % of effects.
analysis.contribution: First national‑scale quantitative causal inference of climate anomalies on hantavirus, providing a GEE‑based framework for a climate‑driven early‑warning system.
analysis.limitations: Only abstract information is available; full methods, validation, and data are not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2025–2026 HPS epidemic in Argentina reached 101 confirmed cases, nearly double the previous season, with the epicenter shifting from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region. Methods: Case data (2020‑2026) with locations were combined via Google Earth Engine with ERA5‑Land temperature, SPI, and soil‑moisture anomalies; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression (INLA) evaluated 1‑6 month lag effects and spatial heterogeneity, and causal mediation analysis tested climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways. Results: Significant spatial clustering (Moran’s I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95 % CrI 1.31‑2.14) at 3‑4 month lag and temperature (RR = 1.45 per °C, 95 % CrI 1.12‑1.87) raised HPS risk, with interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood months increased Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold, drought‑followed‑heavy‑rainfall drove NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2 % and 56.7 % of effects. Contribution: First national‑scale quantitative causal inference of climate anomalies on hantavirus, providing a GEE‑based framework for a climate‑driven early‑warning system. Limitations: Only abstract information is available; full methods, validation, and data are not reported.

papers.title_zh: 气候变化与汉坦病毒在阿根廷的时空相关性分析:基于Google Earth Engine和贝叶斯时空回归的定量因果推断

analysis_zh.background: 2025–2026年阿根廷汉坦病毒肺综合征疫情累计101例确诊,几乎是上一季的两倍,疫情中心从巴塔哥尼亚转移至布宜诺斯艾利斯及NOA地区。
analysis_zh.methods: 将2020‐2026年带有地点信息的病例数据通过Google Earth Engine与ERA5‐Land的温度、SPI和土壤湿度异常相结合;采用贝叶斯时空泊松回归(INLA)评估1‐6个月滞后效应及空间异质性;并使用因果中介分析检验气候‐植被‐啮齿动物‐人类路径。
analysis_zh.contribution: 首次在全国尺度上对气候异常对汉坦病毒的定量因果影响进行推断,提供基于GEE的气候驱动预警系统框架。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅有摘要信息;完整方法、验证及数据未公布。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：2025–2026年阿根廷汉坦病毒肺综合征疫情累计101例确诊,几乎是上一季的两倍,疫情中心从巴塔哥尼亚转移至布宜诺斯艾利斯及NOA地区。 方法：将2020‐2026年带有地点信息的病例数据通过Google Earth Engine与ERA5‐Land的温度、SPI和土壤湿度异常相结合;… 结果：未报告 贡献：首次在全国尺度上对气候异常对汉坦病毒的定量因果影响进行推断,提供基于GEE的气候驱动预警系统框架。 局限：仅有摘要信息;完整方法、验证及数据未公布。

papers.title: Neurological Manifestations of Hantavirus Infection: A Review.
papers.abstract: IMPORTANCE: Hantaviruses have caused several recent human disease outbreaks. They carry a high mortality rate, and some species can spread from person to person although they are typically spread from rodents to humans. Neurological complications have been documented but are poorly understood. OBSERVATIONS: Broadly, 2 types of syndromes have been associated with hantaviruses. The pulmonary syndrome carries a high mortality rate and can result in pulmonary and cardiac failure. Neurological involvement is typically secondary to hypoxic and ischemic injury or metabolic dysfunction. Another manifestation of the infection that is associated with some species of the virus is termed hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. Some patients may develop a meningoencephalitis or pituitary apoplexy resulting in sudden visual loss and pituitary dysfunction. Rarely, seizures, myelitis, and peripheral neuropathy may occur. Although there are no specific antiviral drugs approved for treatment of hantavirus, there are several that showed efficacy in preclinical trials. Management is supportive care and treatment of symptoms. CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: Neurological manifestations of hantavirus infection are uncommon but can be severe. Prospective studies and experimental models are needed to better characterize these manifestations, understand pathophysiology, identify therapeutic targets, and develop guidelines for management.

analysis.background: Hantaviruses have caused recent human disease outbreaks, and their neurological complications are documented but remain poorly understood.
analysis.main_directions: Two clinical syndromes (pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal) are linked to hantavirus; neurological involvement is often secondary to hypoxic/ischemic or metabolic injury, but can include meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, and peripheral neuropathy.
analysis.current_state: Neurological manifestations are uncommon yet can be severe, usually secondary to systemic injury; no specific antivirals are approved and management is supportive care.
analysis.gaps: The pathophysiology, incidence, and optimal therapeutic approaches for hantavirus‑related neurological disease are unclear, and evidence is limited to case observations.
analysis.future_research: Prospective clinical studies and experimental models are needed to clarify mechanisms, identify therapeutic targets, and develop evidence‑based management guidelines.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses have caused recent outbreaks and neurological complications are poorly understood. Main directions: Two syndromes (pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal) are described; neurological disease may be secondary to hypoxia/ischemia or metabolic dysfunction, with occasional meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, or neuropathy. Current state: Neurological manifestations are uncommon but can be severe; they are often secondary to systemic injury, no approved antivirals exist, and care is supportive. Gaps: Pathophysiology and incidence remain unclear, and evidence for specific therapies is lacking. Future research: Prospective clinical studies and experimental models are required to delineate mechanisms, identify therapeutic targets, and develop management guidelines.

papers.title_zh: 关于汉坦病毒感染的神经系统表现:综述。

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒已导致近期人类疾病暴发,已记录其神经系统并发症,但仍缺乏充分了解。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 两种临床综合征(肺部综合征和肾综合征出血热)与汉坦病毒相关;神经系统受累常继发于缺氧/缺血或代谢损伤,但可表现为脑膜脑炎、垂体出血、癫痫发作、脊髓炎和周围神经病变。
analysis_zh.current_state: 神经系统表现虽少见但可严重,通常为全身损伤的继发表现;尚无获批的特异性抗病毒药物,治疗以支持性护理为主。
analysis_zh.gaps: 汉坦病毒相关神经疾病的发病机制、发病率及最佳治疗方案尚不明确,证据仅限于病例观察。
analysis_zh.future_research: 需要前瞻性临床研究和实验模型以阐明机制、确定治疗靶点,并制定循证的管理指南。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒已导致近期人类疾病暴发,已记录其神经系统并发症,但仍缺乏充分了解。 主要方向：两种临床综合征(肺部综合征和肾综合征出血热)与汉坦病毒相关;神经系统受累常继发于缺氧/缺血或代谢损伤,但可表现为脑膜脑炎、垂体出血、癫痫发作、… 研究现状：神经系统表现虽少见但可严重,通常为全身损伤的继发表现;尚无获批的特异性抗病毒药物,治疗以支持性护理为主。 不足：汉坦病毒相关神经疾病的发病机制、发病率及最佳治疗方案尚不明确,证据仅限于病例观察。 后续研究：需要前瞻性临床研究和实验模型以阐明机制、确定治疗靶点,并制定循证的管理指南。

papers.title: Distribution and richness of Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts: Implications for zoonotic risk in Southern South America.
papers.abstract: Orthohantaviruses represent a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America (SSA), where 13 native rodent species are recognized reservoir hosts of 15 different viral genotypes. The information on their geographic distribution remains scattered, limiting regional assessments of exposure risk. We compiled and curated occurrence records for the Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts in SSA and used ecological niche modeling to estimate their potential distributions. We integrated binary presence predictions to map reservoir and genotype richness as well as reservoir assemblages. The richness maps were combined with gridded human population data to estimate the number of people potentially exposed. Most Orthohantavirus reservoir species in SSA are distributed over eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil. Hotspots of reservoir richness were concentrated in the Humid Chaco, while the area of high genotype richness extends across the Chaco-Pampean plain. Exposure to a high diversity of pathogenic genotypes (≥7) was estimated to exceed 13 million people under the most precautionary scenario. This study provides the first integrated, region-wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, offering an updated ecological baseline to support surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies.

analysis.background: Orthohantaviruses are a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America, with 13 native rodent species serving as reservoirs for 15 viral genotypes, but their geographic distribution information is scattered, limiting risk assessment.
analysis.methods: The authors compiled occurrence records for reservoir hosts in SSA and applied ecological niche modeling to predict potential distributions; binary presence predictions were integrated to map reservoir and genotype richness and assemblages, and these richness maps were overlaid with gridded human population data to estimate exposed populations.
analysis.results: Most reservoir species are predicted across eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; hotspots of reservoir richness occur in the Humid Chaco, while high genotype richness spans the Chaco‑Pampean plain; exposure to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes was estimated to affect over 13 million people under the most precautionary scenario.
analysis.contribution: This work provides the first integrated, region‑wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, establishing an ecological baseline to inform surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is provided, so detailed methods, validation, and uncertainty estimates are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Orthohantaviruses pose a major zoonotic risk in Southern South America, with 13 rodent reservoirs for 15 genotypes, yet distribution data are fragmented. Methods: Occurrence records were compiled and ecological niche modeling used to predict host distributions; binary predictions mapped reservoir and genotype richness, which were combined with human population grids to estimate exposure. Results: Reservoirs are mainly in eastern/northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; richness hotspots lie in the Humid Chaco and genotype richness across the Chaco‑Pampean plain; >13 million people may be exposed to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes. Contribution: First region‑wide integrated assessment offers an ecological baseline for surveillance and risk mapping. Limitations: Only the abstract is available, so detailed methodological and uncertainty information is not reported.

papers.title_zh: 正汉坦病毒 储存宿主的分布与丰富度:对南美洲南部人畜共患风险的意义。

analysis_zh.background: 正汉坦病毒es 是南美洲南部的主要人畜共患威胁,13 种本土啮齿动物作为 15 种病毒基因型的储宿主,但其地理分布信息分散,限制了风险评估。
analysis_zh.methods: 作者汇编了南美洲南部(SSA)储宿主的出现记录,并运用生态位模型预测潜在分布;将二元存在预测整合用于绘制储宿主和基因型的丰富度及组合图,并将这些丰富度图与网格化的人口数据叠加,以估算受暴露人口。
analysis_zh.contribution: 本研究首次提供了在南美洲南部对 正汉坦病毒 储宿主分布与丰富度的整体区域评估,建立了生态基线,以指导监测、风险绘图和防控策略。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供了摘要,因而未在所提供的证据中报告详细方法、验证及不确定性估计。

papers.title: Hantavirus on a Cruise Ship: What COVID-19 Taught Us.
papers.abstract: Abstract Delayed recognition of the mechanics of aerosol transmission – the primary mode of spread of SARS-CoV-2 – at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic led to inadequate early recommendations regarding nonpharmaceutical interventions and poor initial messaging about how to reduce infection risk. We highlight key epidemiological details of Andes virus (ANDV) transmission on the MV Hondius cruise ship and during prior reported outbreaks with confirmed person-to-person spread in Argentina, which, together, suggest aerosol spread may be a key mechanism of transmission of ANDV between source cases and susceptible contacts in permissible environmental settings.
papers.journal: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

papers.title: IMMUNOINFORMATICS-GUIDED MULTI-EPITOPE VACCINE CANDIDATE DESIGN AND MHC DOCKING VALIDATION TARGETING THE GLYCOPROTEIN PRECURSOR OF THE NEWLY REPORTED ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 ORTHOHANTAVIRUS
papers.abstract: Abstract The rapid release of viral genomic sequences allows early immunoinformatics screening before experimentally curated antigen information becomes available. In this study, the glycoprotein precursor (GP) of the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus was evaluated as a vaccine-design target using a stepwise workflow that integrated antigenicity prediction, conserved-region screening, transmembrane topology, MHC-I and MHC-II epitope prediction, peptide-construct design, and molecular docking against representative MHC receptors. The full GP sequence was predicted as a probable antigen by VaxiJen with a score of 0.5194. Conserved regions 1-50 and 81-240 were retained as antigenic regions and were located outside the predicted membrane-spanning helices. After filtering by MHC binding score and independent VaxiJen antigenicity, two CTL epitopes (VIYEKTHCV and KVKKTVLCY) and six HTL epitopes were selected. Four multi-epitope constructs (F1-F4) were generated using AAY and GPGPG linkers. Docking showed that F4 had the strongest MHC-I docking score (-226.92), whereas F2 had the strongest MHC-II docking score (-260.14). Residue contact-map analysis further indicated that the strongest complexes were supported by hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, alkyl/aliphatic contacts, and van der Waals interactions. These findings prioritize F2 and F4 as leading candidates for further three-dimensional refinement, molecular dynamics, allergenicity/toxicity filtering, population coverage, and experimental validation.
papers.journal: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Residue contact-map analysis further indicated that the strongest complexes were supported by hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, alkyl/aliphatic contacts, and van der Waals interactions. These findings prioritize F2 and F4 as leading candidates for further three-dimensional refinement, molecular dynamics, allergenicity/toxicity filtering, population coverage, and experimental validation.

source_records.title: IMMUNOINFORMATICS-GUIDED MULTI-EPITOPE VACCINE CANDIDATE DESIGN AND MHC DOCKING VALIDATION TARGETING THE GLYCOPROTEIN PRECURSOR OF THE NEWLY REPORTED ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 ORTHOHANTAVIRUS
source_records.abstract: Abstract The rapid release of viral genomic sequences allows early immunoinformatics screening before experimentally curated antigen information becomes available. In this study, the glycoprotein precursor (GP) of the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus was evaluated as a vaccine-design target using a stepwise workflow that integrated antigenicity prediction, conserved-region screening, transmembrane topology, MHC-I and MHC-II epitope prediction, peptide-construct design, and molecular docking against representative MHC receptors. The full GP sequence was predicted as a probable antigen by VaxiJen with a score of 0.5194. Conserved regions 1-50 and 81-240 were retained as antigenic regions and were located outside the predicted membrane-spanning helices. After filtering by MHC binding score and independent VaxiJen antigenicity, two CTL epitopes (VIYEKTHCV and KVKKTVLCY) and six HTL epitopes were selected. Four multi-epitope constructs (F1-F4) were generated using AAY and GPGPG linkers. Docking showed that F4 had the strongest MHC-I docking score (-226.92), whereas F2 had the strongest MHC-II docking score (-260.14). Residue contact-map analysis further indicated that the strongest complexes were supported by hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, alkyl/aliphatic contacts, and van der Waals interactions. These findings prioritize F2 and F4 as leading candidates for further three-dimensional refinement, molecular dynamics, allergenicity/toxicity filtering, population coverage, and experimental validation.

analysis.background: The rapid release of viral genomic sequences enables early immunoinformatics screening before experimental antigen data are available (A1).
analysis.methods: The study applied a stepwise immunoinformatics workflow to the glycoprotein precursor of the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus, encompassing antigenicity prediction, conserved‑region screening, transmembrane topology assessment, MHC‑I and MHC‑II epitope prediction, peptide‑construct design, and molecular docking to representative MHC receptors (A2).
analysis.results: VaxiJen scored the full GP as a probable antigen (0.5194) (A3); conserved regions 1‑50 and 81‑240 were retained as extracellular antigenic segments (A4); two CTL and six HTL epitopes passed MHC binding and antigenicity filters (A5); four constructs (F1‑F4) were assembled using linkers (A6); docking identified F4 as the strongest MHC‑I binder (score –226.92) and F2 as the strongest MHC‑II binder (score –260.14) (A7); contact‑map analysis showed supporting hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, alkyl/aliphatic contacts, and van der Waals interactions (A8).
analysis.contribution: The work prioritizes constructs F2 and F4 as leading multi‑epitope vaccine candidates for this novel orthohantavirus, informing subsequent 3‑D refinement, molecular dynamics, allergenicity/toxicity filtering, population‑coverage assessment, and experimental validation (A9).
analysis.limitations: Only abstract information is available; findings are limited to in‑silico predictions without experimental validation, and details on sample size, host, or geographic context are not reported (A1).

analysis.summary_en: Background: The rapid release of viral genomes permits early immunoinformatics screening before experimental antigen data exist (A1). Methods: A stepwise workflow evaluated the glycoprotein precursor of the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus, including antigenicity, conserved‑region, transmembrane, MHC‑I/II epitope prediction, construct design, and docking (A2). Results: VaxiJen gave the GP a score of 0.5194 (A3); regions 1‑50 and 81‑240 were selected as extracellular antigenic segments (A4); two CTL and six HTL epitopes passed filters (A5); four constructs (F1‑F4) were built (A6); docking ranked F4 best for MHC‑I (‑226.92) and F2 best for MHC‑II (‑260.14) (A7); contact‑map analysis confirmed stabilizing interactions (A8). Contribution: Constructs F2 and F4 are highlighted as priority vaccine candidates for further refinement and experimental testing (A9). Limitations: The report is abstract‑only, providing only computational results without experimental validation or detailed contextual data (A1).

papers.title_zh: 免疫信息学指导的多表位疫苗候选设计及MHC对接验证,针对新报告的安第斯病毒/瑞士/Hu-3337/2026 糖蛋白前体 正汉坦病毒

analysis_zh.background: 病毒基因组序列的快速发布使得在实验抗原数据可得之前即可进行早期免疫信息学筛选 (A1)。
analysis_zh.methods: 本研究对新报告的安第斯病毒/瑞士/Hu-3337/2026 正汉坦病毒 的糖蛋白前体采用了逐步免疫信息学工作流,包括抗原性预测、保守区筛选、跨膜拓扑评估、MHC‐I 与 MHC‐II 表位预测、肽构建体设计以及与代表性MHC受体的分子对接 (A2)。
analysis_zh.results: VaxiJen 将完整GP评为可能抗原(0.5194) (A3);保守区 1‐50 和 81‐240 被保留为细胞外抗原片段 (A4);两段CTL表位和六段HTL表位通过了MHC结合和抗原性过滤 (A5);使用连接肽组装了四个构建体(F1‐F4) (A6);对接结果显示F4为最强的MHC‐I结合体(得分 –226.92),F2为最强的MHC‐II结合体(得分 –260.14) (A7);接触图分析显示存在氢键、盐桥、烷基/脂肪族接触以及范德华相互作用 (A8)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 这项工作优先考虑构建 F2 和 F4 作为这种新型 正汉坦病毒 的主要多表位候选疫苗,为后续的 3D 细化、分子动力学、过敏性/毒性过滤、人群覆盖率评估和实验验证提供信息 (A9)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅有摘要信息可得;研究结果局限于计算预测,缺乏实验验证,且未报告样本量、宿主或地理背景等细节 (A1)。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:病毒基因组序列的快速发布使得在实验抗原数据可得之前即可进行早期免疫信息学筛选 (A1)。 方法:本研究对新报告的安第斯病毒/瑞士/Hu-3337/2026 正汉坦病毒 的糖蛋白前体采用了逐步... 结果:VaxiJen 将完整GP评为可能抗原(0.5194) (A3);保守区 1‐50 和 81‐... 贡献:这项工作优先考虑构建 F2 和 F4 作为这种新型 正汉坦病毒 的主要多表位候选疫苗,为后续的... 局限:仅有摘要信息可得;研究结果局限于计算预测,缺乏实验验证,且未报告样本量、宿主或地理背景等细节...

papers.title: Hantavirus infections in a changing world: epidemiology, pathogenesis, and one health challenges.
papers.abstract: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics. Transmitted primarily through rodent reservoirs, hantaviruses cause two major clinical syndromes: haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS). Despite being historically confined to specific geographic regions, recent environmental changes, increased human animal interaction, and global mobility have contributed to evolving epidemiological patterns. This review summarises current knowledge on hantavirus transmission, global epidemiology, pathophysiological mechanisms, clinical manifestations, and therapeutic approaches. Particular attention is given to the emerging global epidemiology of Seoul virus (SEOV), an urban-associated hantavirus linked to brown rats and pet rodents. In addition, it explores emerging challenges related to vaccine development, surveillance, and the integration of One Health strategies in the prevention and control of hantavirus infections. Strengthening multidisciplinary approaches and improving early detection remain critical to mitigating the global impact of these infections.

full_text_sections.full_text: Abstract Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics. Transmitted primarily through rodent reservoirs, hantaviruses cause two major clinical syndromes: haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS). Despite being historically confined to specific geographic regions, recent environmental changes, increased human animal interaction, and global mobility have contributed to evolving epidemiological patterns. This review summarises current knowledge on hantavirus transmission, global epidemiology, pathophysiological mechanisms, clinical manifestations, and therapeutic approaches. Particular attention is given to the emerging global epidemiology of Seoul virus (SEOV), an urban-associated hantavirus linked to brown rats and pet rodents. In addition, it explores emerging challenges related to vaccine development, surveillance, and the integration of One Health strategies in the prevention and control of hantavirus infections. Strengthening multidisciplinary approaches and improving early detection remain critical to mitigating the global impact of these infections. analysis.background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance with high morbidity, mortality, and complex ecological dynamics (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review focuses on rodent‑borne transmission, the two major clinical syndromes (HFRS and HCPS) (A2), the emerging urban epidemiology of Seoul virus (A5), and challenges in vaccine development, surveillance, and One Health integration (A6).
analysis.current_state: Current knowledge on transmission pathways, global epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and therapeutic options is summarised (A4).
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include limited vaccine candidates, insufficient surveillance systems, and the need to operationalise One Health approaches (A6), as well as the requirement for stronger multidisciplinary early‑detection frameworks (A7).
analysis.future_research: Future work should strengthen multidisciplinary collaborations, improve early detection and surveillance, and advance vaccine research within a One Health context (A7).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance with high morbidity, mortality, and complex ecological dynamics (A1). Main directions: The review addresses rodent transmission, HFRS and HCPS syndromes (A2), the rising urban spread of Seoul virus (A5), and challenges in vaccines, surveillance, and One Health (A6). Current state: Existing knowledge on transmission, epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical features, and treatment is summarised (A4). Gaps: Limited vaccine options, inadequate surveillance, and incomplete One Health implementation (A6) plus a need for stronger multidisciplinary early‑detection efforts (A7). Future research: Strengthen multidisciplinary approaches, enhance early detection and surveillance, and develop vaccines within a One Health framework (A7).

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 变化中的世界中的感染:流行病学、发病机制与一体化健康挑战。

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒 感染是具有全球重要性的动物源性疾病,具有高发病率、高死亡率以及复杂的生态动态 (A1)。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述聚焦于啮齿动物传播、两大主要临床综合征(肾综合征出血热 (HFRS) 与汉坦病毒心肺综合征 (HCPS)) (A2)、首尔病毒 的新兴城市流行病学 (A5),以及疫苗研发、监测和一体化健康整合的挑战 (A6)。
analysis_zh.current_state: 对传播途径、全球流行病学、发病机制、临床表现及治疗方案的现有认识已作概述 (A4)。
analysis_zh.gaps: 主要不足包括疫苗候选品种有限、监测体系不足以及需要落实一体化健康方法 (A6),同时还需建立更强的多学科早期检测框架 (A7)。
analysis_zh.future_research: 未来工作应加强多学科合作、提升早期检测与监测,并在一体化健康框架下推进疫苗研究 (A7)。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒 感染是具有全球重要性的动物源性疾病,具有高发病率、高死亡率以及复杂的生态动态 (A1)。 主要方向：本综述聚焦于啮齿动物传播、两大主要临床综合征(肾综合征出血热 (HFRS) 与汉坦病毒心肺综合征 (HCPS)) (A2)、首尔病毒 的新兴城… 研究现状：对传播途径、全球流行病学、发病机制、临床表现及治疗方案的现有认识已作概述 (A4)。 不足：主要不足包括疫苗候选品种有限、监测体系不足以及需要落实一体化健康方法 (A6),同时还需建立更强的多学科早期检测框架 (A7)。 后续研究：未来工作应加强多学科合作、提升早期检测与监测,并在一体化健康框架下推进疫苗研究 (A7)。

papers.title: Hantavirus evidence in West and Central Asia: a scoping review protocol with implications for surveillance
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are hosted mainly by rodents and other small mammals. Human infection may present as haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, nephropathia epidemica, hantavirus pulmonary syndrome, or hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome, although mild and non-specific febrile illness also occurs. Patterns of disease and viral circulation depend on the virus and host species, local ecology, human exposure, diagnostic practice, and the organisation of surveillance. Evidence from West and Central Asia is scattered across clinical reports, serological surveys, rodent studies, molecular investigations, and surveillance programmes for undifferentiated febrile illness. The available literature suggests that hantaviruses circulate in parts of Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, but the amount and type of evidence vary markedly between countries. In several settings, detection has relied on a single serological assay, while molecular confirmation and linked human-animal surveillance remain uncommon. Other countries in the region have little or no readily identifiable published evidence. A lack of publications, however, cannot be taken to mean that viral circulation is absent. A scoping review is suited to this heterogeneous literature. The purpose is to establish what has been studied, where evidence has been generated, how infection or circulation has been assessed, and which gaps are most relevant to surveillance.

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are primarily hosted by rodents and other small mammals and can cause a spectrum of human disease from hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome to mild febrile illness.
analysis.main_directions: Disease patterns and viral circulation are shaped by virus‑host ecology, human exposure, diagnostics, and surveillance structures; a scoping review is proposed to map this heterogeneous literature.
analysis.current_state: Published data indicate hantavirus circulation in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, though the depth and type of evidence differ markedly among these countries.
analysis.gaps: Evidence is fragmented across clinical reports, serology, rodent and molecular studies, with many detections based on single serological assays and limited linked human‑animal surveillance; several regional countries have little or no published data.
analysis.future_research: Enhanced surveillance should incorporate molecular confirmation, integrated human‑animal studies, and systematic mapping of existing evidence to identify priority gaps.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are rodent‑borne and cause a range of human illnesses. Main directions: Their ecology, diagnostics, and surveillance drive disease patterns, prompting a scoping review of the scattered literature. Current state: Circulation is documented in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, but evidence quality varies. Gaps: Data are fragmented, often rely on single serology tests, and many countries lack published reports. Future research: Implement molecular confirmation, linked human‑animal surveillance, and comprehensive evidence mapping to guide surveillance priorities.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 在西部和中亚的证据:一项范围审查协议,涉及监测的意义

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒主要寄宿于啮齿动物和其他小型哺乳动物,可导致人类疾病谱从肾综合征出血热到轻度发热性疾病。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 疾病模式和病毒循环受病毒‐宿主生态、人类暴露、诊断以及监测体系的影响;拟开展范围审查以绘制该异质文献的全景。
analysis_zh.current_state: 已发表数据表明汉坦病毒在土耳其、伊朗、哈萨克斯坦和南高加索地区循环,然而各国的证据深度和类型差异显著。
analysis_zh.gaps: 证据在临床报告、血清学、啮齿动物及分子研究中零散,许多检测仅基于单一血清学检测且缺乏人畜关联监测;部分地区国家的已发表数据极少或缺失。
analysis_zh.future_research: 加强监测应纳入分子确认、人与动物一体化研究,并系统绘制现有证据以识别重点空白。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒主要寄宿于啮齿动物和其他小型哺乳动物,可导致人类疾病谱从肾综合征出血热到轻度发热性疾病。 主要方向：疾病模式和病毒循环受病毒‐宿主生态、人类暴露、诊断以及监测体系的影响;拟开展范围审查以绘制该异质文献的全景。 研究现状：已发表数据表明汉坦病毒在土耳其、伊朗、哈萨克斯坦和南高加索地区循环,然而各国的证据深度和类型差异显著。 不足：证据在临床报告、血清学、啮齿动物及分子研究中零散,许多检测仅基于单一血清学检测且缺乏人畜关联监测;部分地区国家的已发表数据极少或缺失。 后续研究：加强监测应纳入分子确认、人与动物一体化研究,并系统绘制现有证据以识别重点空白。

papers.title: ANALYSIS OF GENOTYPE FREQUENCIES OF MONONUCLEOTIDE POLYMORPHISMS OF EARLY IMMUNE RESPONSE GENES IN HFRS PATIENTS
papers.abstract: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) remains one of the leading natural focal infections in Russia, characterized by pronounced clinical variability. Clinical manifestations of HFRS, in particular, may be determined by single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in early immune response genes in patients. We determined the allele and genotype frequencies of SNPs in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‐6 (rs1800795) genes. CASP1 encodes caspase‐1, an enzyme that is the product of the activated inflammasome, while IL‐6 encodes interleukin‐6, a cytokine of the acute immune response. It was found that the genotype frequencies in the control group conformed to the Hardy–Weinberg law, whereas in HFRS patients a statistically significant deviation from the expected equilibrium was detected, which suggests the existence of correlations between the genotypes of the studied SNPs and the clinical manifestations of the disease.

analysis.background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is a leading focal infection in Russia with marked clinical variability.
analysis.methods: The study genotyped single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‑6 (rs1800795) genes in HFRS patients and a control group, using standard allele‑frequency determination techniques.
analysis.results: Genotype frequencies in controls followed Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, whereas HFRS patients showed a statistically significant deviation, indicating potential correlations between these SNPs and disease manifestations.
analysis.contribution: The findings suggest that polymorphisms in early immune‑response genes may influence HFRS clinical outcomes, providing a basis for further genetic surveillance and risk stratification.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is a leading focal infection in Russia with marked clinical variability. Methods: The study genotyped SNPs in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‑6 (rs1800795) genes in HFRS patients and controls. Results: Control genotypes conformed to Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, while HFRS patients showed significant deviation, suggesting correlations. Contribution: Polymorphisms in early immune‑response genes may influence HFRS clinical outcomes, informing surveillance and risk assessment. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

papers.title_zh: HFRS患者早期免疫应答基因单核苷酸多态性基因型频率分析

analysis_zh.background: 肾综合征出血热(HFRS)是俄罗斯主要的局部感染,临床表现差异显著。
analysis_zh.methods: 本研究对HFRS患者及对照组的CASP1(rs580253)和IL-6(rs1800795)基因的单核苷酸多态性(SNP)进行基因分型,采用标准等位基因频率测定技术。
analysis_zh.results: 对照组的基因型频率符合哈迪-温伯格平衡,而HFRS患者出现统计学显著偏离,提示这些SNP可能与疾病表现相关。
analysis_zh.contribution: 研究结果表明,早期免疫应答基因的多态性可能影响HFRS临床结局,为进一步的基因监测和风险分层提供依据。
analysis_zh.limitations: 在提供的证据中未报告。

papers.title: A 5-Adic Ultrametric Framework for Alignment-Free Phylogenetic Analysis of Hantavirus RNA Sequences
papers.abstract: We develop a non-Archimedean framework for the representation and analysis of genomic sequences based on the arithmetic and geometric structure of the ring of 5-adic integers. The proposed approach associates RNA sequences with points in a compact ultrametric space through an injective symbolic-to-arithmetic embedding that transforms genomic information into a hierarchical geometric object. We prove that the embedding is a global isometry between a natural symbolic prefix metric and the induced 5-adic metric, and we show that its image forms a compact Cantor-type subset of Z5. Building upon this representation, we formulate a continuous-time evolutionary model governed by a Vladimirov pseudo-differential operator. The resulting non-Archimedean diffusion equation provides a mathematically rigorous mechanism for describing evolutionary transitions across hierarchical genomic scales and admits an explicit fundamental solution obtained through 5-adic Fourier analysis. We further introduce a finite-resolution projection onto quotient rings of Z5 and develop an alignment-free phylogenetic inference framework based directly on the 5-adic valuation. The induced distance function is ultrametric and naturally encodes hierarchical relationships through shared symbolic prefixes. The proposed construction establishes a bridge between p-adic analysis, ultrametric geometry, pseudo-differential operators, and computational phylogenetics. As an illustration, we discuss its application to Hantavirus genomic sequences, demonstrating how hierarchical evolutionary organization can be represented within a unified non-Archimedean mathematical framework.

analysis.background: The paper addresses the need for alignment‑free phylogenetic analysis of Hantavirus RNA sequences by proposing a novel mathematical framework.
analysis.methods: An injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic embedding maps RNA sequences into a compact ultrametric space of 5‑adic integers (A2); a finite‑resolution projection onto quotient rings of Z5 and a 5‑adic valuation‑based distance are used for phylogenetic inference (A6, A7).
analysis.results: The embedding is proved to be a global isometry linking a symbolic prefix metric to the 5‑adic metric, with its image forming a compact Cantor‑type subset of Z5 (A3); a continuous‑time evolutionary model based on a Vladimirov pseudo‑differential operator yields a diffusion equation with an explicit fundamental solution via 5‑adic Fourier analysis (A4, A5); the induced ultrametric distance naturally encodes hierarchical relationships (A7); the framework is illustrated on Hantavirus genomic sequences (A9).
analysis.contribution: The work bridges p‑adic analysis, ultrametric geometry, pseudo‑differential operators, and computational phylogenetics, providing a mathematically rigorous, alignment‑free approach for hierarchical evolutionary inference (A8, A9).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; no empirical data, performance evaluation, or validation on actual Hantavirus datasets are reported (abstract_only).

analysis.summary_en: Background: The study proposes a new alignment‑free phylogenetic method for Hantavirus RNA using a 5‑adic framework. Methods: RNA sequences are embedded into a 5‑adic ultrametric space via an injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic map and analyzed with a valuation‑based distance (A2, A6, A7). Results: The embedding is a global isometry forming a Cantor‑type subset; a Vladimirov‑operator diffusion model yields an explicit solution; the ultrametric distance captures hierarchical relationships; illustration on Hantavirus sequences demonstrates feasibility (A3‑A5, A7, A9). Contribution: Links p‑adic mathematics to phylogenetics, offering a rigorous, alignment‑free hierarchical inference tool (A8, A9). Limitations: Only abstract information is available; no empirical validation or quantitative performance data are reported.

papers.title_zh: 一种用于无序列比对的 汉坦病毒 RNA 序列系统发育分析的 5‐adic 超度量框架

analysis_zh.background: 本文通过提出一种新颖的数学框架,解决了对 汉坦病毒 RNA 序列进行无比对系统发育分析的需求。
analysis_zh.methods: 一种单射的符号到算术嵌入将 RNA 序列映射到 5‐adic 整数的紧致超度量空间 (A2);通过对 Z5 的商环进行有限分辨率投影并采用基于 5‐adic 估值的距离进行系统发育推断 (A6, A7)。
analysis_zh.results: 证明该嵌入是全局等距映射,将符号前缀度量与 5‐adic 度量关联,其像构成 Z5 的紧致 Cantor 型子集 (A3);基于 Vladimirov 伪微分算子的连续时间进化模型得到一个扩散方程,并通过 5‐adic 傅里叶分析给出显式基本解 (A4, A5);诱导的超度量距离自然编码层级关系 (A7);该框架在 汉坦病毒 基因组序列上进行示例说明 (A9)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该工作将 p‐adic 分析、超度量几何、伪微分算子与计算系统发育学相结合,提供了一种数学上严格、无需比对的层级进化推断方法 (A8, A9)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供摘要;未报告实证数据、性能评估或对真实 汉坦病毒 数据集的验证 (abstract_only)。

papers.title: Watching the Ship, Missing the Forest: Why Ecological Spillover, Reservoir Ecology, and Environmental Change Remain the True Hantavirus Threats in North America.
papers.abstract: Hantavirus emergence in North America is driven by interactions among reservoir ecology, environmental variability, landscape conditions, and human exposure, with ongoing environmental change increasing spillover opportunities, altering the geography of risk, and challenging traditional public health surveillance systems.

papers.title: Serologic Evidence of Hantavirus Exposure in Humans, Italy, 2004-2018.
papers.abstract: Rodentborne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed. We tested samples collected from 371 patients during 2004-2018 and detected LCMV IgG in 2.7% of samples and hantavirus IgG in 7.3%. Puumala virus-neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 cases, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation.

We tested samples collected from 371 patients during 2004-2018 and detected LCMV IgG in 2.7% of samples and hantavirus IgG in 7.3%. Puumala virus-neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 cases, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation.
