papers.title: 2026 Cruise Ship-associated Andes Hantavirus Outbreak and Public Health Response.
papers.abstract: OBJECTIVES: Andes virus (ANDV) is an exceptional hantavirus for which human-to-human transmission has been repeatedly reported, and it causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with high lethality in South America. This review aims to summarize the epidemiological and clinical features of hantavirus infections and their transmission routes, and to examine transmission patterns within a confined, high-density environment and future public health response strategies, using the 2026 ANDV outbreak on the MV Hondius cruise ship as a case study. METHODS: Drawing on publicly available reports from the World Health Organization (WHO), the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control, the Pan American Health Organization, and the Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency, as well as relevant literature, we reviewed the characteristics of hantavirus infections and this outbreak. RESULTS: The MV Hondius departed from Ushuaia, Argentina, on April 1, 2026. On May 2, the UK International Health Regulations (IHR) National Focal Point formally alerted the WHO to a cluster of severe acute respiratory illnesses. By May 27, 13 individuals (11 confirmed and 2 probable cases) had been infected, and 3 had died. Genomic analysis revealed no more than one single-nucleotide polymorphism difference among patients, indicating onboard human-to-human transmission following a single or a very small number of zoonotic spillover events; the effective reproduction number was estimated to be 0.7 on May 22. Major factors contributing to transmission included advanced age (mean, approximately 65 years), shared cabins, communal indoor spaces, and close caregiving or clinical contact. CONCLUSIONS: This outbreak demonstrates that pathogens with limited human-to-human transmissibility can nevertheless be amplified in a closed, high-density environment. Prompt identification, international coordination under the IHR, and efficient risk communication were central to controlling the outbreak, and preparedness for potential importation requires routine assessment of travel history, surveillance for suspected cases, and access to rapid diagnostic testing.

Prompt identification, international coordination under the IHR, and efficient risk communication were central to controlling the outbreak, and preparedness for potential importation requires routine assessment of travel history, surveillance for suspected cases, and access to rapid diagnostic testing.

source_records.title: 2026 Cruise Ship-associated Andes Hantavirus Outbreak and Public Health Response.
source_records.abstract: Objectives Andes virus (ANDV) is an exceptional hantavirus for which human-to-human transmission has been repeatedly reported, and it causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with high lethality in South America. This review aims to summarize the epidemiological and clinical features of hantavirus infections and their transmission routes, and to examine transmission patterns within a confined, high-density environment and future public health response strategies, using the 2026 ANDV outbreak on the MV Hondius cruise ship as a case study. Methods Drawing on publicly available reports from the World Health Organization (WHO), the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control, the Pan American Health Organization, and the Korea Disease Control and Prevention Agency, as well as relevant literature, we reviewed the characteristics of hantavirus infections and this outbreak. Results The MV Hondius departed from Ushuaia, Argentina, on April 1, 2026. On May 2, the UK International Health Regulations (IHR) National Focal Point formally alerted the WHO to a cluster of severe acute respiratory illnesses. By May 27, 13 individuals (11 confirmed and 2 probable cases) had been infected, and 3 had died. Genomic analysis revealed no more than one single-nucleotide polymorphism difference among patients, indicating onboard human-to-human transmission following a single or a very small number of zoonotic spillover events; the effective reproduction number was estimated to be 0.7 on May 22. Major factors contributing to transmission included advanced age (mean, approximately 65 years), shared cabins, communal indoor spaces, and close caregiving or clinical contact. Conclusions This outbreak demonstrates that pathogens with limited human-to-human transmissibility can nevertheless be amplified in a closed, high-density environment. Prompt identification, international coordination under the IHR, and efficient risk communication were central to controlling the outbreak, and preparedness for potential importation requires routine assessment of travel history, surveillance for suspected cases, and access to rapid diagnostic testing.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely exhibits repeated human‑to‑human transmission and causes severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with high lethality in South America.
analysis.main_directions: The review synthesizes epidemiological and clinical features, transmission routes, and patterns of spread in a confined, high‑density setting, using the 2026 MV Hondius cruise‑ship outbreak as a case study to inform future public‑health response strategies.
analysis.current_state: Genomic sequencing showed ≤1 SNP difference among cases, indicating a single or few zoonotic introductions followed by onboard human‑to‑human spread, with an estimated effective reproduction number of 0.7.
analysis.gaps: Uncertainty remains about the precise conditions that enable limited‑transmissibility pathogens to amplify in closed environments and the generalizability of these findings beyond this single outbreak.
analysis.future_research: Enhanced preparedness should include systematic travel‑history assessment, active surveillance for suspected cases, and deployment of rapid diagnostic testing to detect and contain similar events.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus uniquely transmits between humans and causes lethal cardiopulmonary syndrome. Main directions: The review collates epidemiology, clinical features, transmission routes, and confined‑environment dynamics, using the 2026 cruise‑ship outbreak as a case study for future response. Current state: Genomic data show ≤1 SNP difference among cases, supporting a single or few spillovers with an R ≈ 0.7. Gaps: Conditions that allow limited‑transmissibility pathogens to amplify in closed settings remain unclear and may not generalize. Future research: Strengthen preparedness via travel‑history screening, active case surveillance, and rapid diagnostics.

papers.title_zh: 2026年邮轮相关安第斯病毒汉坦病毒爆发与公共卫生应对

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 独特地表现出人际间反复传播,并在南美洲导致严重 汉坦病毒心肺综合征,致死率高。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述综合了在封闭、高密度环境中的流行病学与临床特征、传播途径及传播模式,利用2026年MV Hondius邮轮爆发案例,为未来公共卫生应对策略提供参考。
analysis_zh.current_state: 基因组测序显示病例间SNP差异≤1,表明单次或少数动物源引入后在船上发生人-人传播,估计有效再生数为0.7。
analysis_zh.gaps: 仍不确定哪些精确条件使得传染性有限的病原体在封闭环境中放大,以及这些发现是否可推广至单一爆发之外。
analysis_zh.future_research: 加强准备工作应包括系统化旅行史评估、对疑似病例的主动监测,以及快速诊断检测的部署,以发现并遏制类似事件。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 独特地表现出人际间反复传播,并在南美洲导致严重 汉坦病毒心肺综合征,... 主要方向:本综述综合了在封闭、高密度环境中的流行病学与临床特征、传播途径及传播模式,利用2026年MV... 研究现状:基因组测序显示病例间SNP差异≤1,表明单次或少数动物源引入后在船上发生人-人传播,估计有效再... 不足:仍不确定哪些精确条件使得传染性有限的病原体在封闭环境中放大,以及这些发现是否可推广至单一爆发之... 后续研究:加强准备工作应包括系统化旅行史评估、对疑似病例的主动监测,以及快速诊断检测的部署,以发现并遏制...
